mishap.hexboard.bad_operation: Execute board operation failed, caused by: %s, %s
page.hexboard.board_operation.0: These five patterns are added by HexBoard. $(br)Common rule: argument #1 is always a $(bold)vector$() pointing to any block in the target connected board.
page.hexboard.board_operation.load: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = start slot index, $(br)3) any/list = iota source. $(br)Load writes from the start index; if arg #3 is not a List Iota, it is treated as a single-element list.
page.hexboard.board_operation.delete: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Delete removes all slots in [l, r]; reversed bounds are normalized automatically.
page.hexboard.board_operation.move: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index, $(br)4) number = destination start index. $(br)Move relocates [left, right] to target if the destination range is legal.
page.hexboard.board_operation.resolve: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Resolve evaluates the selected range and returns one List Iota containing the result sequence.
page.hexboard.board_operation.literal_re: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Literal Resolve uses literal behavior on the same range and also returns one List Iota.
page.hexboard.board.0: Boards are where your typeblocks live. A single board contributes 16 slots, and adjacent boards with the same facing connect into one larger workspace.
page.hexboard.board.1: To place content quickly, use typeblocks directly on a board slot, or use the Staff For Board for precise editing. The highlighted slot in HUD is your current target.
page.hexboard.board.2: Connected boards use one global slot order: rows are processed from higher world Y to lower world Y, and inside the same row, slots are read left-to-right relative to board facing.
page.hexboard.board_staff.0: Staff For Board is the primary editor for board workflows. Right-click performs the current mode's normal action, and Shift + right-click performs its confirm action. Hold Shift and scroll to cycle modes, or bind dedicated mode keys in controls.
page.hexboard.board_staff.1: $(bold)Add$(): right-click opens pattern drawing and creates a Quartz Type Block at target.$(br)$(bold)Delete$(): right-click sets selection bounds; Shift + right-click deletes either the selected range or the target slot when no valid range is set.
page.hexboard.board_staff.2: $(bold)Move$(): right-click sets selection, Shift + right-click moves that range to target if destination is valid.$(br)$(bold)Copy$(): same selection flow, but duplicates instead of moving. Both modes preview legality with board overlay colors before confirm.
page.hexboard.board_staff.3: $(bold)Resolve$(): resolves the selected range and writes to a writable Iota holder in your hands.$(br)$(bold)Literal Resolve$(): performs literal resolve on the same selected range.$(br)Both Resolve and Literal Resolve always produce a $(bold)List Iota$().$(br)$(bold)Load$(): Shift + right-click reads from your held focus/holder and inserts converted typeblocks from target; it normally consumes a List Iota, and if the input is not a List Iota, it is treated as a single-element list.
page.hexboard.board_staff.4: HUD details (current mode, target index, and target typeblock info) require Scrying Sight, usually from wearing a Scrying Lens. Even without it, world-space board outlines still show target/selection and valid/invalid operation previews.
page.hexboard.patchouli_integration.0: When the HUD can identify a pattern on a typeblock, it also shows a prompt for opening the matching Hex Book entry.
page.hexboard.patchouli_integration.1: Use the keybind shown in that prompt (default: R) to jump directly to the related spell page in The Hex Book.
page.hexboard.patchouli_integration.2: On pattern pages with stroke-order components, hold Ctrl/Alt/Shift and click the pattern to place it directly onto your board target as a Quartz Type Block. For pages with two patterns, left and right mouse buttons choose different pattern entries.
page.hexboard.quartz_typeblock.0: Quartz Type Block stores Pattern iotas and is the standard way to write spell patterns onto a board. Add mode also creates this type directly.
page.hexboard.quartz_typeblock.1: Quartz blocks are writeable and can be overwritten. During Resolve and Literal Resolve, their stored iota is emitted into the result list.
page.hexboard.wool_typeblock.0: Wool Type Block is a general container for iotas that do not map to specialized blocks. Load mode uses it as the fallback output type.
page.hexboard.wool_typeblock.1: Wool blocks are write-once by design: once written, they cannot be overwritten directly.
page.hexboard.lapis_typeblock.0: Lapis Type Block stores macro definitions (it can also accept patterns, which are converted into macro definitions). A macro definition is a special iota that only takes effect in board logic.
page.hexboard.lapis_typeblock.1: Use lapis blocks as anchors for macro sections. During normal Resolve they define callable blocks; during Literal Resolve they output their own stored macro-definition iota.$(br)In general, the selected range for normal Resolve should not include lapis type blocks.
page.hexboard.redstone_typeblock.0: Redstone Type Block stores macro calls. Its pattern selects which lapis-defined section should be executed.
page.hexboard.redstone_typeblock.1: During normal Resolve, a redstone block searches matching lapis definitions (not necessarily inside the selected range) and executes subsequent non-lapis slots; during Literal Resolve, it outputs only its own macro-call iota.
page.hexboard.redstone_typeblock.2: Example: Slot 2 is a Lapis definition for pattern A; slots 3-5 are its body. Slot 8 is a Redstone call with pattern A. On Resolve, slot 8 finds slot 2 and executes slots 3-5 in order. If no matching lapis definition exists, nothing is expanded.
